era_context: The early 11th century CE is a period of significant political, religious, and cultural change across Eurasia. The Fatimid Caliphate is at its height under al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah, the Song Dynasty is consolidating its power in China, the Chola Empire is expanding in South India, and feudal kingdoms are emerging in Europe. This is also a period of religious ferment, with the Christian Church approaching the Great Schism and Islamic scholarship flourishing.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and the Americas (minimal written records)

events.title: Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah's religious policies intensify in the Fatimid Caliphate
events.description: Caliph al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah intensified his campaign of religious intolerance and confiscation of Christian and Jewish properties throughout the Fatimid Caliphate in 1008 CE. These policies, which would culminate in the destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in 1011 CE, were part of a broader effort to reassert Islamic orthodoxy and centralize Fatimid authority. The persecution caused significant social upheaval and contributed to the migration of Christian and Jewish communities within the Caliphate.

sources.name: Al-Maqrizi, Itti'az al-Hunafa bi-Akhbar al-A'imma al-Khulafa

events.certainty_note: Al-Hakim's persecution of religious minorities is well-attested in multiple sources. The specific events of 1008 CE are less clearly documented than the destruction of the Holy Sepulchre in 1011 CE, but the broader campaign is confirmed.
cross_references: Precedes the destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre (1011 CE)

events.title: Rajaraja I of the Chola Empire dies
events.description: Rajaraja I, one of the greatest Chola emperors, died in 1008 CE (some sources place his death in 1014 CE). Rajaraja I's reign (985–1014 CE) was marked by extensive military conquests, the expansion of Chola power across South India and into Sri Lanka, and the patronage of art and architecture, including the construction of the Brihadeeswarar Temple. His death marked the end of an era of Chola expansion and the beginning of his son Rajendra Chola I's reign, which would see the empire reach its territorial zenith.

sources.name: Inscriptions on the Brihadeeswarar Temple and other temples (epigraphic evidence)

events.certainty_note: Rajaraja I's death is attested in inscriptional evidence and Tamil literary sources. However, the exact date is uncertain; some sources place his death in 1008 CE, while others cite 1014 CE. The discrepancy may reflect different calendar systems or confusion in later sources.
cross_references: Marks the end of Rajaraja I's reign (985–1014 CE)
Leads to Rajendra Chola I's reign (1014–1044 CE)

events.title: Zhenzong Emperor of Song China continues consolidation
events.description: Emperor Zhenzong of the Song Dynasty continued to consolidate his power and authority in 1008 CE, following the Treaty of Chanyuan (1005 CE) with the Liao Dynasty. Zhenzong's reign is marked by cultural and economic flourishing, the patronage of scholarship and the arts, and the development of a sophisticated bureaucratic system. The stability achieved through the treaty with Liao allowed Zhenzong to focus on internal governance and cultural advancement.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)
Song Shi (Official History of the Song Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Zhenzong's reign and consolidation of power are well-documented in Chinese historical records. The year 1008 CE falls within the middle of his reign (997–1022 CE).
cross_references: Follows from the Treaty of Chanyuan (1005 CE)
Part of the Song Dynasty's cultural and economic golden age (10th–11th centuries)

events.title: Cnut II (Canute the Great) consolidates control over England and Scandinavia
events.region: North Sea Empire / England, Denmark, Norway
events.description: Cnut II (Canute the Great) continued to consolidate his control over England, Denmark, and Norway in 1008 CE. Cnut's reign is marked by the integration of Danish and Anglo-Saxon administrative systems, legal reforms, and the establishment of a stable North Sea empire. Cnut's consolidation of power was facilitated by his military victories and his ability to maintain order across his diverse realms.
key_figures: Cnut II / Canute the Great (King of England, Denmark, and Norway)
events.certainty_note: Cnut's consolidation of power is well-documented in contemporary English chronicles. The year 1008 CE falls within the period of his growing influence and consolidation.
cross_references: Part of Cnut's rise to power (1010s–1020s CE)
Leads to Cnut's reign as King of England (1016–1035 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Rajaraja I's death is disputed. Some sources place his death in 1008 CE, while others cite 1014 CE. The discrepancy may reflect different calendar systems or confusion in later sources. Inscriptional evidence from the Brihadeeswarar Temple suggests that the temple was completed or neared completion around 1010 CE, which would be consistent with Rajaraja I's death in 1014 CE rather than 1008 CE. However, some scholars argue that Rajaraja I may have died earlier, and that the temple was completed by his son Rajendra Chola I. The exact date of Rajaraja I's death remains uncertain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1008 CE is moderately rich for East Asia and the Fatimid Caliphate, but sparse for Northern Europe and the Americas. European sources are dominated by later sagas and chronicles, which are often unreliable for precise dating. Islamic sources provide good coverage of the Fatimid Caliphate and the Mediterranean region. Chinese sources are comprehensive for the Song Dynasty. South Indian sources (inscriptions and later Tamil literary works) provide information about the Chola Empire, but the dating of events is sometimes uncertain. The dating of events in this period is complicated by the use of multiple calendar systems and the conversion between them.

graph_edges.note: Rajaraja I's death in 1008 CE (or 1014 CE) led to the succession of his son Rajendra Chola I, who would expand the Chola Empire to its territorial zenith.
Al-Hakim's intensified religious policies in 1008 CE escalated to the destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in 1011 CE.
